title: Border Patrol Kills U.S. Citizen Alex Pretti During Minneapolis Protest
excerpt: U.S. Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse at the Minneapolis VA, during an immigration enforcement operation. Bystander videos contradicted federal claims that Pretti had brandished a weapon or threatened officers.
content:
On January 24, 2026, Border Patrol agents fatally shot Alex Jeffrey Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse for the Department of Veterans Affairs, at the intersection of 26th Street and Nicollet Avenue in Minneapolis — less than three weeks after the killing of Renée Good by an ICE agent in the same city.

  • Federal officials, including DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and Border Patrol Commander Gregory Bovino, immediately framed the shooting as self-defense, claiming Pretti had approached agents with a firearm and intended to "massacre law enforcement."
  • Multiple bystander videos and at least two sworn witness affidavits contradicted those claims, showing Pretti recording agents and being tackled to the ground before being shot at least ten times.
  • A government report to Congress confirmed two agents discharged their weapons but did not state that Pretti brandished his firearm — nor did it document a chain of custody for the weapon, which was placed on a car seat rather than secured in an evidence bag.
  • Federal authorities blocked Minnesota state investigators from accessing the scene and seized evidence, prompting Hennepin County Attorney Mary Moriarty to file an emergency lawsuit for a temporary restraining order to preserve evidence.
  • Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O'Hara confirmed Pretti had no criminal record and held a valid Minnesota permit to carry a firearm.
"Once again DHS has come out with a predetermined narrative that contradicts everything we saw with our own eyes," said U.S. Rep. Kelly Morrison (D-MN).

The killing — the second fatal shooting by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis in 17 days — prompted a nationwide DHS funding crisis in the Senate, as Democrats and some Republicans refused to approve DHS appropriations without accountability measures including mandatory body cameras and de-escalation training requirements.
